The Maritime deck comprises fifty cards organised into five ensigns and three colours: these are gold, bucklers, staves, jewels and arrows. !Maritime ensigns.png Many of the cards furthermore have conventional titles separate to their rank and ensign—for example, the deuce of staves is called the Duel, while the sept of jewels is known as the Stars. Over time the artwork on these cards has come to incorporate these thematic elements. In games where some cards trump the rest, the gold ensign typically takes this role, although some more modern games will use the aforementioned “named” cards in this way.

The use of a Maritime deck for divination mirrors many other sources of randomness, although the modern occult practices only date back to the early nineteenth century. They appear to have been popularised by Promethean clubs associated with the Deviance movement, and specifically the storied Winchester group.

Early records of the specific rules of card games using the Maritime deck are unfortunately thin on the ground. The first real description of a particular game appears in 1555 court records regarding the gambling debts of one Marc Levosclin. ## Sources - Crossing the Rubicon: a Hidden History of Games, a 1982 work of popular history written (originally in Borlish as Y Rubicon Trasir: un Stoir Scos de Joy) by writer and stage performer Jonnovar Meyaunot.

title: Redstone Chair
html:
The Redstone Chair (Merch Redstoan Sete /ˈɾɛd.stoːn ˈsiːt/) was a wooden throne used by the monarchies of Albion during the medieval period. ## History The Redstone Chair was fashioned in the late tenth century around a block of sandstone; the name of the carpenter is forgotten. This block was (allegedly) the block upon which the Elved Marriage between a princess of Markland and the ruler of Dane Northumber was solemnised, ending hostilities between the two polities for almost a century.

Many subsequent monarchs of Albion, and especially of Markland and Northumber, were crowned on the Redstone Chair. During the Second Tetrarchy of the eleventh and twelfth centuries, most of the Drengot Emperors sat upon the Chair, and as such it was relocated to London. After the Empire’s collapse, this placed the Chair in the politically turbulent region between Markland and Kent. Some historians have conjectured that the First Burning of London in 1290 was in part motivated by a plan to acquire the Chair; the responsible party varies in the telling.

According to the traditional account, it was a dispute over the location of the Redstone Chair which sparked the Wars of Fealty, which lasted almost fifty years over the turn of the sixteenth century and which embroiled almost all the polities of Albion.

Unfortunately, the Chair was lost in the tumult of the wars at some point between 1505 and 1509. Despite several searches and at least two hoaxes, the Chair has never been recovered, and the modern understanding of its appearance rests entirely on depictions in a few portraits and coronation scenes.

title: Borrhatic League
html:
The Borrhatic League is a collection of charitable organisations and political action groups with a focus on laic governance, education and (in recent decades) also pacifism and objection. The League grew from collaboration between working-class groups of minority faiths across Northern Europe in the early nineteenth century; the name derives from the Attic Greek Βορρᾶς • Borrhâs “the North Wind”. The League first rose to prominences on the scale of polities during the Second German War of the 1820s and in its aftermath. For instance, Hilgart and Michael Bervisson were influential in Borland’s Borrhatic League at this time, especially with regards to calls for religious tolerance

Although the Borrhatic League and the contemporaneous Deviance movement has (occasionally explosive) disagreements, both are known to have contributed verbiage to the 1850 Laic Declarations in Belgrade. Later in the nineteenth century the Borrhatic League funded orphanages and schools across the region, such as the Borrhatic School of History in the south-eastern Borlish city of Combert, which exists to this day.

title: Brethin
html:
The Brethin are a Christian religious order devoted to record-keeping and the maintenance of history. They have societies worldwide and a history dating back to the early medieval period.

The Brethin take their name from the distinctive heavy cloak (in Old Welsh, the bretin) worn by all members.

The order was founded in eighth-century Tremonnow—at that time a border town of the new Kingdom of Greater Devon—under the patronage of Iseld, daughter of Vigo the Magnificent. Their original mandate was to preserve in particular the history of the Christianisation of Albion and Ireland, but the scope of their work quickly expanded to include secular history.

Brethin houses spread rapidly throughout the Briton states, and in later centuries they made inroads into France, Burgundy and as far afield as Andalus (although they would not gain a foothold in Italy until after the Great Fracture of the sixteenth century).

Not long after its inception, Brethin houses included both monasteries and convents, although in some regions institutions for one sex or the other predominated.

Of particular note are the Brethin of Kernow; they became the most influential school within the order during the late medieval period, with a substantial increase in membership through the twelfth century. At this time many courts of Europe employed monks of the Brethin of Kernow as advisors.

After the medieval period and until the modern day, the Brethin ran several major printing operations, such as the Yievle Brethin Primers, and educational institutions, such as the Tremonow Open School (formerly the Brethin Mesh Institute).

Notable members

  • Pope Damian VI (the Wise), reigned 1199-1209 N
  • Friar Dewock Barclythe, 1452-1539 N
  • Saint Victor of Dunkirk, d. 1245 N

title: Convoy Australier
html:
The Convoy Australier (Merch /ˈkɔn.vəi əˈstɾɛː.liːə/), officially the Order of Saint Hemma of Still Water, is a Christian organisation aligned with Rome which emerged in the Second Tetrarchy period. It has undergone several major transformations of purpose since its inception. ## History

The Convoy began in the eleventh century in the Duchy of Crain, a member of the German Empire subordinate to Bavarn. In the first decades of its existence the Convoy was in essence a (quite literally) glorified band of mercenaries.

The Convoy was heavily involved in the first crusades and related ventures, from the Principality of Mystra in Greece to the unsuccessful forays in Jerusalem. It was appointed to administrate the new states of Antioch and Port Simon soon after their conquests in the twelfth century.

Pope Just revoked the Convoy’s rulership of Antioch and Port Simon in 1208 as the culmination of a escalating inter-faction struggle. However, it is generally agreed that this act saved the Convoy a great amount of trouble in the long run (given the imminent disaster of the Failed Crusade). The Convoy was subsequently assigned to the courts of Svonimir of Crovatia and Taxon of Awry; it was thus ideally placed to lead the Baptism of the North, even as the troubles of the German Secession loomed.

The German Secession increased the urgency of the Baptism of the North; Rome feared that Augsburg might spread its new heresy faster than Rome could reach the North. As a consequence, the Convoy grew substantially in numbers and wealth during the thirteenth century, and outlying chapters were founded as far afield as Borland and (eventually) Lithow.

title: Cordin Editions
html:
Cordin Editions (Borlish Edition Cordin /ˌe.diˈdzjɔn kɔrˈdɪn/) is a publishing house founded in the mid-twentieth century and based in the city of Cordin on Borland.

The company, which is affiliated with the University of Cordin, is recognised for producing educational books for national schools. It also published syllabi covering schooling throughout childhood which were taken up as a standard by most schools in the polity.

Publications

  • The Boral Province in the century after Hadrian (Y Provinç Boral par y siecr posc Hadrian, 1952), written by late classical historian Robiaut Scarvon
  • The Story of Borland (Y Stoir d’Istr Boral, first edition 1982, second edition 1993), a school history textbook
  • Shepherds of Hambrick: the Small History of Dane Borland (Acr a Sauð Vellig, 2008), written by Sconet Ydreç about Borland in the tenth century
  • From Tablets to Touchscreens: The Story of Education (Des Delt vars Dois: Y Stoir d’Education, 2016), written by Theign Roufler in the style of an academic textbook but intended to be read by a large lay audience

title: Dijon New School
html:
The Dijon New School is the principal institute of higher education in Dijon. The School has a department of Concurrence History.

Lecturers

  • Marc Jeichi (fl. 2019), lecturer in concurrence history in peripatetico from the University of Cordin
  • Vanne Romaçue (fl. 2019), lecturer in concurrence history

Courses

  • “Family, Tradition and Language: the Changing Culture of the Good Game Decades”, 2019 course in concurrence history, lectured by Professor Marc Jeichi and Doctor Vanne Romaçue

title: Fiellas Dimenja
html:
Fiellas Dimenja (English: Sunday Sheets) is a weekly newspaper almost continuously published from Marsella since its incorporation in 1871. The paper’s notional remit comprises entertainment and recreational news, although in practise it has regularly covered weightier subjects.

History

Fiellas Dimenja was established in 1871 by Corsican businessman Toscobardo Matutte in concert with the Guiraud Colloquium.

Fiellas was an early adopter of machinal broadcast direct to homes via their tachygraphs, first offering digests in this form in the 1920s.

The paper developed a significant international readership through the twentieth century—by 1934 it was republished synchronously in Vascony and New Provence, and was even read in Collusion territory, despite the rising tensions of the post-Millstone Europe.

By 1950 it held the record for the longest uninterrupted weekly mass-market publication, without interruption by the 1937 Veldsvindung nor the Millstone War (although it briefly moved its printers to Manòsca during the worst of the bombings).

Fiellas’ renown grew further after its significant role in the Furore of 1961, which (not undeservedly) earned the paper a reputation for investigative journalism alongside its ostensible purpose of delivering entertainment and recreational news.

Correspondents

  • Jacint Brunat (fl. 1950), reviewer of books and films

For the best experience I recommend you spend the afternoon drinking Empordan and singing as many songs of glory as you can think of. [The Ascendant (1934)]

This will be no classic: come only for the views (of beautiful forests, and a bevy of handsome actors, I concede) and two hours of mindless tragedy. [Zachariah (1950)]

title: Jacobin Fraternity
html:
The Jacobin Fraternity (Borlish Fraterntað Jagovin), historically also known as the Order of Saint James, is a religious organisation based in the city of Axbane on the south coast of Borland.

The Fraternity were founded in tenth-century Flanders under the patronage of Amelia of Bruges. In the eleventh century they were implicated in a local popular uprising against Drengot Emperor Geoffry II and forced to flee over the sea to Borland. There they built the Peninsular Monastery which still overlooks Axbane.

Funds from the Fraternity contributed to the Jacobin Estates on the outskirts of Damvath, which on their completion in the late eighteenth century became the fixed location of Borland’s parliament.

Today the organisation is known mostly for the guild they own, Jaibos Breweries, as well as for owning significant lands in the city (including the site of one of the ancient Roman bathhouses there).

title: New Vithor University
html:
The New Vithor University (Borlish Nou Universtað a Viðor) is a major academic institution located in the Borlish city of Vithor.

The Vithor New School had been founded by the fifteenth century; by the nineteenth century it was known under the name “New Vithor University”. By 1844 N it included the Vithor Historical Institute (l’Edifiç Historic); said institute was eventually divided into the Faculty of Concurrence History and the Faculty of Domain History.

At some point the New Vithor Primers (Nou Premour a Viðor), producer of academical publications based in the school, was established; in 2007 it was producing the textbooks for the school’s courses.

Alumni

  • Lambort Darrian (fl. c17), philosopher of altruism and charity
  • Michael Bervisson (17th June 1799–12th January 1849), political historian
  • Darren Brodus (fl. 1828), mathematician and pioneer in sam theory
  • Sconet Ydreç (fl. 2008), author and medievalist

Offered courses

  • Chocolate and Rubber: Cappatia during the Intertwining Century (Scoclat e Coscou: Cappatia par y Kintanç Mondial); 1986
  • Concurrence History of the 18th Century: Gold, Science, and Waging War (Histoir Concorrent, s18: Aur, Theory, e l’Art d’Abat); 2007
  • Concurrence History of the 16th Century: Death, Ships and the Movement of the Heavens (Histoir Concorrent, s16: Mort, Vascel eð y Motion Celest); 2008

title: Roman Papacy
html:
The Papacy in Rome is the spiritual seat of much of Christendom.

Despite intermittent conflicts with the Greek patriarchs and several abortive antipope lineages, the Roman Papacy remained the ultimate authority over all Christendom until the German Secession of the early thirteen century. This created a lasting competing seat in the Augsburg See which did not recognise the Roman Papacy’s legitimacy.

The Great Fracture of the mid-sixteenth century severely diminished the temporal powers of the Papacy. While on paper the Pope in Rome remained an absolute ruler (along with political dominion over several smaller Italian and German provinces), in practice the new Triunvirato had political dominion over the city of Rome. ## People - Mercury II (fl. 590), who took a particular interest in Borland as a stronghold of Christianity in the north - Agatho (1001–1026), who canonised David, patron saint of Wales - Nuntius (fl. ca. 1100), who instituted a reform of the Julian calendar and moved the New Year to the 1st of March - Damian V (1110-1121), born Humbert of Ravenna in 1047 - Damian VI (1199–1208), called ‘the Wise’, and first member of the Brethin in the role - Just (1208–), who called for a renewed Crusade for Jerusalem in 1210 (known to history as the Failed Crusade) - Hermeneus II (fl. ca. 1250), more totalist than his predecessors in reaction to the German Secession and instigator of the Baptism of the North

title: University of Cordin
html: The University of Cordin is an institute of higher learning in the city of Cordin on Borland. It is affiliated with the publishing house Cordin Editions, through which it publishes academic textbooks. The School has a faculty of Concurrence History. ## People - Marc Jeichi (fl. 2019), lecturer in concurrence history

title: Astraphor
html:
Astraphor (also called barric force) is the set of phenomena associated with the presence and movement of plentiful substances, or the thrift that one can harness from such movement. ## Etymology The word ‘astraphor’ was coined in the seventeenth century to describe the theoretical source of the sparks certain substances (amber, fur, etc.) would create in darkness after being rubbed. It derives from Ancient Greek roots ἀστρᾰπή (astrapḗ) ‘lightning’ and -φόρος (-phóros) “bearing”; although the expected form would be ‘astrapophor’, even in the earliest texts we see only the shorter word (possibly due to transcription error).

The modifying form ‘barric’ derives from parallel work, and originates in Arabic بَرْق⁩ (barq) “lightning”. ## History The deep theoretical connection between astraphor and light was explored thoroughly in the mid-nineteenth century; the unimaginatively titled magnum opus Astraphor and Light by Merch theorist Wexter Hathwall managed to fully characterise the mechanics of astraphor in a single equation.

At this time, the earlier flag-lamp steeplemesh was in the process of being displaced by barric meshes. Places without a pre-existing extensive flag-lamp system were in many cases faster to adopt the barric mesh; for example, by 1870 the world’s longest wired post line on land spanned the length of Lower Mendeva.

The first undersea barric lines were laid in the 1850s in Kent (Dover to Cales), and from Texel in Willemy to Axbane in Borland, each distances of less than twenty leagues. By the following century hundreds of such lines crossed the world’s oceans.

In the 1950s, the Mortar coalition in Portingale funded research into using threshold force to produce astraphor so as to reduce dependence on imports from the Drengot Collusion; by the 1960s the polity was a world pioneer in constructing threshold mills, becoming a net exporter of astraphor in 1971.

title: CD
html:
Cozebdo-diamaschal benjamine (as a medicine sold under several names, including “CD” or “Tommy pills”) is a hebetant substance developed in the early twentieth century. It was the result of collaboration between the Branwen Sanatory in New Leudong and the University of Rimack in Tavancy, which sought to develop hebetants with faster action and milder adversions. It was first synthesised in 1932, and a decade later it had surpassed salix derivatives in sales worldwide, due to providing better pain relief (as opposed to merely reducing the symptoms of fever). ## Name The name of this substance comprises three parts.

The core of the substance is a benjamine ring; the name “benjamine” is borrowed via Scholastic Latin and Vascon from Arabic لُبَان جَاوِيّ‎ • lubān jāwiyy, literally “Jawan frankincense”, corrupted at some point by the name Benjamin.

The first of the extensions in this substance is a cozebdone branch. Cozebdone spirit is a rearrangement of the linear zebdone spirit, a colourless and flammable air with a spine of four coke atoms. The root zebdo- “four coke atoms” derives from Cairene Arabic زبدة • zébda “butter”, first used when zebdone acid was isolated from milk fat.

The second of the extensions is located directly opposite the first, as indicated by the prefix dia- “across, over”, from Greek. It consists of a maschale branch, which is the acid oil with a spine of length three, a malodorous clear liquid. The substance was named in 1850 after the Ancient Greek μᾰσχᾰ́λη • maschálē “armpit”, since it was isolated from human sweat excreted by skin zoia.

title: Dime measures
html:
The dime measures (less commonly known as the ‘tithe measures’ or ‘tenth measures’) are a modern system of units based on factors of ten. The name derives from the French dîme “one-tenth”, from synonymous decimus in Latin.

The dime measures were codified during the nineteenth century for ease of communication as theoretical development coalesced into a global endeavour. They have been updated several times since, as it has become feasible to use more convenient yardsticks.

Standard modifiers may be added to each unit to increase or decrease their size by factors of 10⁴. In most common use are: - great (10⁴) - tithe (10⁻¹) - petty (10⁻⁴) - scant (10⁻⁸) ## Length There are three bases for dime lengths: the inch, the foot (10 inches) and the league (10⁴ feet). At first, the length of the foot was fixed somewhat arbitrarily as the midpoint of the very slightly discrepant Roman foot and the Cathayan “surveyor’s span”. In 1946, the foot was redefined to make the normal speed (denoted œ) precisely ten great leagues per second.

Several other traditional measures of length were given dime values. For instance, the dime mile is equal to five thousand feet or half a league. ## Heft There are four bases for dime hefts: the scruple, the ounce (100 scruples), the tabre (100 ounces) and the ton (100 tabres). The third of these units takes its approximate value from the Persian mann and its name from the city of Tabris. The ounce was originally defined to be the mass of a cube of water of side length one inch; this was later refined to specify that the water be at its triple point of pressure and temperature. ## Derived units Many units are defined in terms of these base units of length and heft, as well as the second. For example, the shove measures the might required to give a heft of one tabre an acceleration of 1 foot per second per second.

–

1 second 1 second
1 inch 2.99792458 centimetres
1 foot 29.9792458 centimetres
1 league 2.99792458 kilometres
1 scruple 269.383 milligrams
1 ounce 26.9383 grams
1 tabre 2.69383 kilograms
1 ton 269.383 kilograms
1 shove 0.80759 newtons

title: Film
html: The first then-called “house of odyssey” to sell tickets to the public on a regular basis was established in Boxa in 1912. One of the venue’s first great successes was a recording of a ballet concert accompanied by the St Hurroco Youth Orchestra of Furlain.

title: Grimmer
html:
A grimmer (Merch /ˈgɾɪ.mə/, Borlish grimmour /gʀɪˈmuʀ/), formerly and formally an augrimmer, is a device that can be given methods to carry out sequences of arithmetic or logical operations. These methods enable grimmers to perform a wide range of tasks. Grimmers are the substrate of the pistol mesh, which links together billions of such devices.

Calculating devices from the earliest abaci prefigure the grimmer, but its true potential (the ability to perform essentially arbitrary computation, given the correct instructions) arose from theoretic discussions on the possibility of self-replication. In the eighteenth-century, Rachel Desselut argued that in order for living matter to duplicate, its “Form” must contain a readable “Method” describing its construction. In this way she predicted the form-method separation essential to modern grimmer operation. ## Etymology The word grimmer is a clipping of augrimmer, literally “one who calculates, one who performs mathematical computation”; the word referred to a profession until the mid-twentieth century. This derives from noun augrim “computation, calculation”, which originally meant “calculation using Arabic numerals” in particular, and which was a loan via French augrisme from Medieval Latin algorismus. This reflects the name of Persian mathematician al-Khwarismi, whose works introduced the positional number system to Europe. ## References - Parable of the Mute Bookbinder, 1743 philosophical treatise by noblewoman Rachel Desselut - The Grimmer Hall: Developments in Early Methodics, 1999 scholastic quire written by New Leudong historian and methodist Cai Sonquo during his tenure at the University of Mehannin

title: Methodics
html:
Methodics is the field of study pertaining to the discovery, development and improvement of methods. In this scholastic context a method is any series of instructions that may be unambiguously and repeatably followed to produce some result.

History

For millennia methods were developed sporadically without much awareness of general methodical principles. For example, an early method in the historical record is Euclid’s procedure for finding the coarsest scale common to two whole numbers. Later we find methods involving tables of pre-computed values, such as Nadim’s Method for multiplication by means of the Trapolitan identity for arrhumb.

Methods with more immediate practical use were continually developed; by the sixteenth century the threepoint method for calculating distances was vital in creating accurate maps for the explorations of the Intertwining Century.

One the first major results in abstract methodics came from the study of life, with Rachel Desselut’s 1743 work Parable of the Mute Bookbinder, which argued that in order for living matter to duplicate, it must contain within itself an explicit method describing its construction. Although heredian acid would not be discovered for another century, her work accurately predicted the necessary separation of Form and Method that would become the bedrock of the study of heredity.

An increasingly-important category of method from the eighteenth-century onward has been procedures for encrypting text, either to conceal it or else simply to convert it into a suitable form for a given steeplepost.

References

  • Drawing in the Sand: the History of Calculation, a collaborative reference book originally published 1954 in Napolitan as Scheme ne Rene: a Storia d’Amiradanza by the Agenza Scholaria Fodgesa

title: Pistol Mesh
html:
The pistol mesh (Borlish sar pistol), also referred to simply as the mesh, is the global system of long-distance communication which employs a single normalised method for transmitting messages from one endpoint grimmer to another.

Etymology

The phrase “pistol mesh” (in the Roun French form mail pistre) was first used by writer Ashford Pasquier in his 1968 parachthon work Starsail, describing technology that could transmit written messages, sounds and moving images between people in different star systems.

The phrase literally means “network for messages”: the word pistol reflects post-Classical Latin pistola “epistle, written decree”, aphæretic form of synonymous epistola; the word mesh descends from Old English masc “net”.

History

The pistol mesh grew out of several earlier technologies, all the way back to the first flag-lamp steeplepost lines in the eighteenth century allowing short texts to be sent between signal towers.

A century later sees the emergence of tachygraphs, devices allowing one to type text directly onto the barric steeplepost from the comfort of one’s home. However, it was only with the rise of general grimmers in the twentieth century that we start to recognise the modern pistol mesh.

By the late twentieth century, the pistol mesh was sufficiently mature that whole enterprises could be conducted using it as a medium. For example, at this time the Tremonow Open School (formerly the Brethin Mesh Institute) offered history courses across Albion entirely via mesh.

title: Steeplepost Tapestry
html:
The Steeplepost Tapestry (Vascon: Lo Tapìs Campanaral) was a screen of several myriad black or white tiles built in Ambrosia’s capital city of Aiga d’Angoixa. Completed in 1820 and erected to overlook one of the city’s central squares, it was inspired by the (fairly new) ‘latticework’ art movement.

One ambitious goal for the Tapestry was to be able to portray journalist’s sketches of events from across the country with great speed, using the flag-lamp steeplemesh infrastructure that had been built up over the last few decades. Although this was achieved several times, the practical limitations of transmitting so much information by steeplepost prevented it from becoming routine. Instead, the majority of the tapestry’s time was spent displaying short pieces of news or government messages in text and simple symbols.

The use of the word ‘diced’ to mean ‘blocky, pixellated’ dates back to a calque of Provincial icon dau ‘die image’. This phrasing was coined in 1821 in disparaging reference to the Tapestry.

Just a few years into its operation, a series of hoax messages which spread rapidly via the Ambrosian steeplemesh made their way onto the Tapestry. These so-called ‘Lies of 1824’ included a report of the governor’s death and culminated after several days with an alleged declaration of war from New Provence.

In 2020, a replica of the Tapestry was unveiled in Markland to mark the bicentenary of the original’s completion. This occurred as part of a long-running project recreating historical events and monuments in other polities.

title: Steeplepost
html:
Steeplepost refers to any of a group of similar technologies (flag-lamp, barrick, longform, gyrer, etc.) used for transmitting messages over long distances.

Development

The very earliest incarnations of the steeplemesh arose during the Democratic Wars of the mid-eighteenth century, but these tended to be single lines of temporary signal towers set up to span terrain that made it too costly to simply send a rider with a message. Moreover, these were set up only to transmit simple messages; for example, an hour to launch an attack.

These early wartime towers usually relied on lampfire and thus were reliable only at night, or else a collection of patterned flags, which were usable at day but required a shorter distance between watchtowers. The two-arm system, allowing up to nine separate states to be transmitted, was first tested in Thynen (a town in Willemy), where the church’s weathervane was reportedly commandeered to send the first lines of the Pater Noster to the neighbouring town Bost. (Hence, probably, the name “steeplepost”.)

By 1820 Ambrosia had unveiled its Steeplepost Tapestry in Aiga d’Angoixa, which promised to allow the (very slow) transmission of images along the lines by communicating the brightness of each pip in an enormous grid, in the manner of a cross-stitch. It was on the Tapestry that the infamous Lies of 1824 were first displayed.

The very first cross-sea steeplepost lines were established around the same time, with air-steeples in the Rustigh Strait connecting Borland to Willemy during the 1830s. This link would last only until 1854, however, when it was replaced with a barrick line.

The middle of the nineteenth century saw the flag-lamp system gradually superseded by barrick lines generally. Uptake was uneven owing to the twin constraints of geography and vested interests; for example, Mendevan powers tended to embrace the new technology more enthusiastically than their similarly-situated Vetomundine counterparts.

The new lines made partially-automated transmission much easier to develop. A prototype wired tachygraph, the Vilmanas Teleclavia, first successfully sent a message via the steeplemesh in 1859.

Encoding

Steeplescripts, or methods by which a message is translated into a transmissible form, have many forerunners in secret codes used for spycraft and warfare. For example, Markland under King Andrew III during the Wars of Fealty employed “Thieves’ Script” to send secret messages, converting each letter or common word into a series of three symbols.

One early line in Andalus (from Cordova to Málaga, usually given as the first line connecting two major cities, and essentially just formalising the signal system that had existed for the duration of their conflict with Morrack) used a variant of the ancient Polybius square adapted for Arabic script.

The first modern steeplescript was developed in Cracow by engineer Sigmund Adamich and his son Andrei as part of the royal initiative to connect Cracow to Bitom, using a set of four symbols, traditionally referred to as “dot, dash, bar and hook”.

Cultural Detaxion

The spread of the steeplemesh reduced overland communication times extremely rapidly (by the end of the nineteenth century some places boasted thrice-daily post delivery, not to mentioned the nascent wired tachygraphs), and undersea barrick links would further connect the world.

Steeplemesh may have contributed to the decline of the voidtale fashion, demystifying formerly-unknowable distant lands and weakening the cultural narrative of the pioneering documentarian explorer.

title: Psolic aphlox
html:
Psolic aphlox (Merch /ˈsɔ.lɪk ˈa.flɔks/) is a colourless, odourless and toxic air formed by burning organic material without enough ignifex.

Though the noxious effects of breathing the air in closed spaces where a coal fire is burning have been known since antiquity (and likely since prehistory), truly theoretical investigation did not begin until the seventeenth century, and the air was not isolated from the acid oil palaquate until the Long Peace period at the turn of the nineteenth century. Its modern name, first used in 1854, comprises the adjective psolic from Greek ψόλος • psólos “soot, smoke” and the noun aphlox from Greek ᾰ̓φλόξ • ăphlóx “without flame”.

title: Sandrine
html:
Sandrine (Merch /ˈsan.dɾəin/) is a soluble substance found in citrus and other fruits and vegetables. It is one of the substances essential to mammal life; a severe defect of sandrine is what causes scurvy.

(Do not confuse this substance with mandrine, a deadly poison.)

The curative effects of citrus fruits for scurvy were suspected as early as the fourteenth century. By the late fifteenth and early sixteenth centuries, with the much longer geoscopic voyages following the Novomundine Landfall of 1471, efforts to prevent scurvy became more active. For example, King Ambrose III of Vascony (the ‘Ascendant’) instituted the Sandrine Design in 1485 by which ships would be required to stock dried fruits and syrups. This scheme, named for his sister Alexandra and the first European royal to set foot in the Novomund, is where the name sandrine originates.

However, such schemes did not have lasting effects. This was partly due to expense but much more significantly, due to the lack of understanding of the mechanism by which fruit prevented scurvy. Thus, fruit would be preserved in ways which removed their sandrine content (for example, boiling or use of copper vessels) and sailors would suffer scurvy anyway, leading guilds to abandon stocking fruits altogether.

Truly theoretic understanding would not come until the seventeenth century, when it was discovered by chance that the guy-rabbit can also develop scurvy (humans and guys are rare among animals in not creating sandrine in our bodies from sugar). This allowed for much more rigorous and time-efficient testing on guys, to determine what preparations of fruits and vegetables were effective at preventing scurvy. By the 1700s it had become common practice to stock lemon juice diluted with strong alcohol on long sea voyages.

title: Tachygraph
html:
A tachygraph (Merch /ˌta.kɪˈgɾaf/, also informally a tachy or tacky /ˈta.kəi/) is a device with a keyboard for typing text onto paper, or more recently directly onto a slate. ## Etymology The word “tachygraph” originates in the mid-nineteenth century as a brand name. It is derived from Ancient Greek τᾰχῠ́ς • (takhús) “quick, fast” and γρᾰ́φω • (gráphō) “draw, write”. ## History Early tachygraphs in the 1830s and 40s were extremely cumbersome, with most models only capable of setting type at a fraction of the speed of practised handwriting. The Armanburg Tachygraph (Tachygrafia Armangrod), which was commercially available in Poland from 1842, was so awkward to use that it was lampooned in the bit-sheets as the Bradygrafia (literally the “slow-writer”).

Since very early on, attempts have been made to connect tachygraphs to the steeplemesh, so that one could type a message onto paper and simultaneously transmit a copy of the message to someone else (ideally, automating a tachygraph to type the message out also on the receiving end).

The first tachygraph specifically able to send messages on the steeplemesh was the Vilmanas Teleclavia, designed in Lithow by Tomas Vilmanas in 1859. Its first message (the first line from Ovid’s Heroines, reproduced below) was sent on Michaelmas of that year to his friend Margreta Legaite in Tvaniste.

· A B C D E F G · HAEC TUA PENELOPE LENTO TIBI MITTIT ULISSE · a b c d e f g. This your Penelope sends to you, long-delayed Ulysses.

Historical films have made much of the fact that this message was sent less than a month after the 1859 Auroric Anomaly, but there is no evidence Vilmanas considered its effects (though he must have been aware of the stories of messages along unpowered steeplepost wires).

In the late nineteenth century, the Faxim brand of tachygraph was sold across Borland.

title: Threshold force
html:
Threshold force is the force derived from splitting the nuxes of atoms. The name ‘threshold’ refers to the so-called ‘threshold heft’ of an unstable substance like killdark, above which one has a self-sustaining ‘chain detaxion’. # History The story of threshold force begins in 1902, with the discovery of the atomic nux—that is, the discovery that almost all of the heft of an atom is located near its centre. This took place at the Nÿstad Atellier (then privately owned and run by substantial theorist Lohic Courvoisier de Nÿstad) in the Cappatian city of Varonøw.

The first threshold mills capable of supplying force for general use were in operation by the 1960s. These, and the unsettling martial applications of threshold force which had already been demonstrated on a small scale, brought the concept of harmful shortform luxes into the popular consciousness. Many a blacklayer film of the period and thereafter features unstoppable invisible rays inflicting horrors on the characters.

The corporal used in the vast majority of threshold mills, killdark, was first isolated in the mid-nineteenth century in the Irish shire of Killdara (whence, via Welsh kildárc ‘from Killdara’, the substance’s name).

Portingale was a pioneer of threshold force, funding research into its effectivity in the 1950s in a bid to reduce their dependence on astraphor exported by the Drengot Collusion. The polity’s efforts were repaid in full; Portingale became a net exporter of astraphor in the 1970s.

References

  • Lux Æterna: The First Century of Threshold Force, written in 2002 by Cawen ba Dirxen

title: Vulgar Latin to Modern Borlish
html:
The dialects of Borlish are the only surviving descendants of the Insular variety of Vulgar Latin, which comprised Borlish Latin and British Latin. This variety was not overly divergent from the contemporary (ca. 3rd to 5th century) Latin of the continent, with the following exceptions: ## Insular Latin Innovations

  1. Loss of intervocalic /w/, as evidenced in inscriptions:
    • clam “key” (continental clavem)
    • denio “I reach” (continental devenio)
  2. Loss of word-final /s/, probably preceded by a period of debuccalisation where final /s/ was pronounced /h/. The dating of this sound change is controversial:
    • Fifth-century a femna “the women (acc.)” (Classical hās fēminās “these women”)

The second of these local changes was grammatically significant, as it weakened the distinctions between forms in noun declensions. Indeed, already by the Old Borlish period all number and case distinctions in nouns have been lost. ## Historical Sound Changes

1. Posttonic Vowel Loss & Final Syllable Simplification

  • V → ∅ / ˈVC_CV (posttonic vowel loss)
  • m → n / _# if stressed
  • m s → ∅ / _# otherwise

Example: amictate /ˌa.mikˈta.te/ “friendship” ← amīcitātem ### 2. Palatalisation Part 1 - [t k]j [d g]j → tʃ dʒ - k g → tʃ dʒ / _[i e] - k → j / _C except [r l w j] ### 3. Fortition - w → β / #_ - j → dʒ / #_, V_V Example: iazo /ˈdʒat.tʃo/ “I lie down” ← iaceō ### 4. Consonant Lenition Part 1 - p t k b d g s → b d g β ð ɣ / V_[V m l r n j w] ### 5. Palatalisation Part 2 - nj lj → ɲ ʎ - Cj → Cʲ → jC except w or word initially ### 6. Final Vowel Reduction - V → ə / _# unless stressed Examples: - sabe /ˈsa.bə/ “he knows” ← sapit - saibe /ˈsaj.bə/ “I know” ← sapiō ### 7. Weak Consonants Lost - [β ð ɣ] → ∅ / V_V ### 8. Mid-Vowel Breaking - ɛ e ɔ o → je e o ow / stressed open - ɛ ɔ → e o / elsewhere ### 9. Lenition Part 2 - p t k b d g → b d g β ð ɣ / V_V - C: → C (degemination) ### 10. Loss of Final Schwa - ə → ∅ except after consonant clusters Example: liei /ljedʒ/ “halt” ← Insular laedicum “collision” ### 11. Affricate Shift - tʃ dʒ → ts ʒ ### 12. sk Palatalisation & β Shift - sk β → ʃ v ### 13. Vowel Fronting - u → y ### 14. Mid-Vowel Rhotic Shift - e → a / _rC - e → i / _r# ### 15. Full Loss of Schwa - ə → ∅ ### 16. Epenthesis Before r - ∅ → d / [n ɲ]_r - ∅ → b / m_r Examples: - cendre /ˈtsen.dr̩/ “ash” ← cinerem - puðir /pyˈðir/ “rot” ← pūtēre ### 17. Vowel Laxing - i e o y → ɪ ɛ ɔ ʏ / closed syllables ### 18. Final Consonant Devoicing - b d g v z ð ʒ → p t k f s θ ʃ / _# ### 19. l-Vocalisation & y-Diphthong Shift - [l y] → w / _[C #] ### 20. Front Vowel Mergers - [ɛ ɪ ʏ ɛj] → ɪ / #_sC - [ɛ ɪ ʏ] → ɛ / _w[C #] ### 21. Diphthong Shift Part 1 - ai ɛi ɪi → e i əj - aw ɛw ɔw → o əw u ### 22. Loss of Palatals and ɣ - ɲ ʎ → n l / #_ - ɲ ʎ ɣ → jn jl j Examples: - aut /ot/ “had” ← awt ← ayt ← ayd ← aud ← aˈβu.də ← aˈbu.tə ← aˈbu.to ← Vulgar habūtus - ismargre /ɪsˈmar.gr̩/ “arise” ← ɛjsˈmar.grə ← ejsˈmer.grə ← ɛksˈmɛr.gre ← Vulgar exmergere ## Late Old Borlish (ca. 1200)

This is the stage, around the turn of the thirteenth century, usually referred to as late Old Borlish:

…sovravnt il deft nostre saȝntaðesem eð atavalesem n iȝ atrevre golfhavn seȝ hamar dont y verb divin ismetre ac povre paian. peðev soul ez font istovent por vn nov cliȝs d istroienz istablir…

IPA: /soˈvront ɪl dɛwt ˈnostr̩ ˌsajn.taˈðɛsɛm ɛð aˌta.vaˈlɛsɛm nəj aˈtrəwr̩ gɔlfˈhon sej haˈmar dɔnt i vɛrb diˈvɪn ɪsˈme.tr̩ ak ˈpovr̩ paˈʒan ‖ peˈðəw sul ets fɔnt ɪs.toˈvɛnt pɔr yn nu cləjs dɪsˈtrɔjənts is.taˈblɪr/

Translation: …uphold our most sacred and ancient duty to let Gulfhaven be the centre from which we will send the Word of God to pagan lands. We ask only for the necessary funds for a new teachinghouse… ## Middle Borlish Period

23. Rhotacisation of Syllabic l

  • l → r / C_# except [j w]_# This gives Borlish its signature pairs like:
  • capabr “competent”
  • capablessem “more competent” ### 24. Aphæresis of Initial isC
  • ɪ → ∅ / #_sC

Like IRL Italian, this means that many words gain an initial vowel only to lose it a millennium later: - Latin stannum “tin” → Old Borlish istan “tin” → Modern Borlish stan “tin” However, this process also deletes initial vowels that were there from the start: - Latin historia “story” → Old Borlish istoir “story” → Modern Borlish stoir “story” ### 25. Break-up of sr Clusters & Hiatus Resolution - ∅ → t / s_r - ∅ → v / [ɔ o u]_V - uv → ov The second rule is particularly interesting, since it regenerates some ⟨v⟩ which had been lost in the time of Insular Latin: - Latin novellus “new” → Insular noellus “new” → Old Borlish noel “new” → Modern Borlish novel “new” ### 26. Loss of Front Rounded Vowels - y ʏ → i ɪ This provides Borlish with another signature feature, namely ⟨u⟩ /i ɪ/: - tu /ti/ “you (sg)” - prun /prɪn/ “plum” The loss of front rounded vowels led to some homophonous pairs like lun lin /lɪn/ “moon; flax”. In many cases this led to one of the pair falling out of use (in this case the latter was replaced with the competing form linsc). ### 27. Voicing of Final s - s → z / _# The precise timing of this sound change is hard to determine, since it does not interact with any nearby sound changes. Likely this originated as a sandhi effect before a following vowel-initial word, e.g. tu es un /ti ɛ‿zɪn/ “you are a…”, before being extended to all contexts. ## Modern Period: Diphthong Shift Part 2 (1600-1800)

28. The Great Diphthong Shift

  • aj ej ij → ɛj i aj
  • əj əw → aj aw Borlish’s answer to the IRL Great Vowel Shift, these changes are somewhat more modest (and take place a little later, between 1600 and 1800). In combination with the loss of front rounded vowels, however, these changes determine much of Borlish’s least intuitive orthographic behaviour. Example: muy /maj/ “crumb”, where ⟨uy⟩ underwent the changes /yj → ij → aj/. Note: The previous shift reflects the outcomes in the Damvath Standard dialect of Borlish; these recent changes differ greatly in applicability across the island. ## Late Sound Shift (1800s)

29. Late Consonant Backing

  • ʃ r → x ʀ The latter of these appears to be part of a general areal phenomenon of backing rhotics. A single word reflecting both these changes is scuir /xajʀ/ “boy”.

title: Adjectives
html:
Borlish adjectives have two forms: a base form and a comparative form. The comparative is formed by adding a suffix, which shifts stress to the penultimate syllable. ### Regular Comparative in -essem

The regular comparative suffix is -essem, which replaces or follows the final segment of the adjective and attracts penultimate stress. ‹leger legeressem› /leˈʒɛr ˌle.ʒeˈrɛ.sɛm/ ‘light lighter’

Several orthographic adjustments may occur when the suffix is added. Before the vowel-initial suffix, ‹ç› becomes ‹c›, while ‹c› becomes ‹ch› to preserve the /k/ pronunciation. ‹lavaç lavacessem | starc starchessem› /laˈvats ˌla.vaˈtsɛ.sɛm | stark starˈkɛ.sɛm/ ‘wasteful {more wasteful} | strong stronger’

After a final orthographic ‹-q›, a linking ‹-u-› is inserted. ‹picq picquessem› /pɪk pɪˈkwɛ.sɛm/ ‘small smaller’

The final consonant may be doubled in the comparative form to maintain the checked quality of the preceding vowel. This occurs most commonly with final ‹-l›. ‹boncop boncoppessem› /bɔnˈkɔp ˌbɔn.kɔˈpɛ.sɛm/ ‘cheap cheaper’

The final consonant may be voiced in the comparative form. This is seen most commonly in present participles ending in ‹-nt› (which form comparatives in ‹-ndessem›) and in adjectives ending in fricatives, though only final ‹-f› reflects this orthographically as ‹-vessem›. ‹tart tardessem | braf bravessem› /tart tarˈdɛ.sɛm | braf braˈvɛ.sɛm/ ‘late later | brave braver’

Adjectives ending in a written ‹-Vu› diphthong form the comparative in ‹-Vvessem›. ‹blau blavessem› /blo blaˈvɛ.sɛm/ ‘blue bluer’

Certain historically long vowels in the final syllable of an adjective may shorten in the comparative form. This occurs most commonly with adjectives ending in ‹-ous›. ‹fatuous fatuosessem› /faˈtjuz ˌfa.tjoˈzɛ.sɛm/ ‘clumsy clumsier’

Adjectives ending in ‹-e› form the comparative in ‹-eyessem›. ‹idone idoneyessem› /ˌi.doˈne ˌi.doˈnjɛ.sɛm/ ‘convenient {more convenient}’

Adjectives in Syllabic -r

Adjectives ending in syllabic ‹-r› show several patterns. Occasionally they form regular comparatives in ‹-ressem›. ‹jundr jundressem› /ˈʒɪn.dr̩ ʒɪnˈdrɛ.sɛm/ ‘junior {more junior}’

Some adjectives, most notably those ending in the suffix ‹-abr› “-able”, form comparatives in ‹-lessem› rather than ‹-ressem›. ‹stabr stablessem | sengr senglessem› /ˈstab.r̩ staˈblɛ.sɛm | ˈsɛn.gr̩ sɛnˈglɛ.sɛm/ ‘steady steadier | simple simpler’

The majority of adjectives ending in syllabic ‹-r› form the comparative with the suffix ‹-errem›, which replaces the final syllabic consonant and is stressed penultimately. The same adjustments that affect ‹-essem› may also affect ‹-errem›. ‹nuvr nuverrem | diçr dicerrem› /ˈnɪv.r̩ niˈvɛ.rɛm | ˈdɪts.r̩ diˈtsɛ.rɛm/ ‘fresh fresher | great greater’

‹allagr allagherrem leur leverrem›
/aˈlɛj.r̩ ˌa.lɛˈjɛ.rɛm ˈlau.r̩ leˈvɛ.rɛm/
‘happy happier free freer’

Irregular Comparatives

A few adjectives show irregular stem changes in the comparative. ‹brey bressem› /bri ˈbrɛ.sɛm/ ‘short shorter’

Several common adjectives have fully suppletive comparative forms. ‹bon ottem | mal pessem | gran maissem› /bɔn ˈɔ.tɛm | mal ˈpɛ.sɛm | gran ˈme.sɛm/ ‘good better | bad worse | big bigger’

title: Allophony
html:
Throughout this section, the phonetic transcription approximately reflects the mesolectal speech in the capital city of Damvath. ### Assimilation The phoneme /n/ is pronounced [ŋ] before velars. ‹blanc viking mensc› /blank viˈkɪng mɛnx/ [blaŋk vɪˈkɪŋg mɛŋx] ‘white raid honour’

Regressive voicing assimilation affects /s/ before any voiced obstruent, nasal or /l/. ‹sboc svanið pasnagl desjeun bruslaç› /sbɔk svaˈnɪθ paˈsnɛjl dɛsˈʒaun briˈslats/ [zbɔk zvɐˈnɪθ pɐˈznɛjl dɪzˈʝaun bʀɪˈzlats] ‘smirk unconscious parsnip breakfast sunburn’ ### Palatals The phoneme /x/ surfaces as [ç] when adjacent to a front vowel. ‹scið esc› /xɪθ ɛx/ [çɪθ ɛç] ‘possible bait’

The phoneme /ʒ/, along with /j/ either word-initially or between an unstressed and a stressed vowel (in that order), is pronounced [ʝ]. ‹jast yon çuyal› /ʒast jɔn tsaˈjal/ [ʝast ʝɔn tsɐˈʝal] ‘zinc where cicada’ ### Non-rhoticity Syllabic /r̩/ surfaces as [ɐ]. Furthermore, for some speakers syllabic /m n/ surface as [ɐm ɐn] respectively. ‹issambr ðesmbal visn› /ɪˈsamb.r̩ ˌðɛz.m̩ˈbal vɪz.n̩/ [ɪˈsam.bɐ ˌðɛ.zɐmˈbal ˈvɪ.zɐn] ‘massacre dodgeball knot’

Coda /r/ surfaces as length on a preceding monophthong, or as [ɐ] after a diphthong or /j/. The vowel’s quality may also be altered, with /ar ir ur/ becoming [ɐː ɪː ʊː] respectively. ‹vert hagr tourm› /vɛrt ˈhɛj.r̩ turm/ [vɛːt ˈhɛ.jɐ tʊːm] ‘green linchpin squadron’

When a word beginning with a vowel immediately follows, the [ʀ] is resurrected as the onset of the next syllable. ‹l’hour uncos› /lur ɪnˈkɔz/ [lʊː‿ ʀɪŋˈkɔz] ‘df=time something’ ### Reduction In syllables without primary or secondary stress, the vowel /a/ surfaces as [ɐ], the vowels /ɛ e i/ as [ɪ] and the vowels /ɔ o u/ as [ʊ]. ‹tovaresc taisson torvegl› /ˌto.vaˈrɛx teˈsɔn tɔrˈvɪjl/ [ˌto.vɐˈʀɛç tɪˈsɔn tʊːˈvɪjl] ‘homosexual badger whirlwind’

Before an obstruent or after /n/, a coda /ts/ is pronounced [s]. ‹amaçgat fenç› /ˌa.matsˈgat fɛnts/ [ˌa.mɐsˈgat fɛns] ‘nursery split’

The sequence /kts/ is realised as [ks]. ‹acceir facçon› /akˈtsir fakˈtsɔn/ [ɐkˈsɪː fɐkˈsɔn] ‘approach party’

title: Alphabet
html:
Here are the twenty-seven letters of the standard Borlish alphabet, along with their letter names:

Letter Name Letter Name Letter Name
a a h hac q cu
b be i i r ar
c ce j jot s es
ç cedil k ka t te
d de l el u iscon
ð eð m em v ve
e e n en x ex
f ef o o y oy
g ge p pe z zet

Special Characters

The character ‹w›, which is used in foreign words and some names (like Willaum ‘William’), is pronounced like ‹v› and called vescon ‘second-vee’. There are also some compound characters. The ligatures ‹æ œ›, primarily used in Classical loanwords, are called æsc and œthel respectively and pronounced as if written ‹e›. The character ‹ï› is referred to as i jammel ‘twin i’ and pronounced as if written ‹yi›.

title: Consonants
html:
Note: ‹y› is considered a vowel when written between consonants and/or word boundaries, and a consonant otherwise.

Consonants

The following letters regularly denote a single phoneme.

Letter Phoneme Letter Phoneme
b /b/ m /m/
ç /ts/ n /n/
d /d/ p /p/
f /f/ q /k/
j /ʒ/ r /r/
k /k/ v /v/
l /l/ z /z/

Of these, the letters ‹b d f l m n p r› may appear doubled, which usually does not affect their pronunciation. ‹abbað joddy affis ollom commet bannir çoppin arrum› /aˈbaθ ʒɔˈdi aˈfɪz ɔˈlɔm kɔˈmɛt baˈnɪr tsɔˈpɪn aˈrɪm/ ‘abbot cuppa tip relic ban start toffee cosine’

The only exception to this is that ‹mm nn rr› may denote a single consonant followed by a syllabic consonant. This is the reading when ‹mm nn rr› occurs word-finally or before a consonant. ‹yem-m grin-n car-r› /ˈjɛm.m̩ ˈgrɪn.n̩ ˈkar.r̩/ ‘get-1p judge-3p seek-inf’

The letter ‹ð› is pronounced /θ/ word-finally and /ð/ otherwise. ‹arð naðr ðeu› /arθ ˈnað.r̩ ðau/ ‘steep viper buttocks’

The letter ‹x› regularly denotes the phoneme sequence /ks/. ‹xivol euxon incox› /ksiˈvɔl auˈksɔn ɪnˈkɔks/ ‘waltz quartz digestion’

Consider the final six letters ‹c g h s t y› when they occur alone (not part of a digraph). In this case, the letters ‹t y› regularly denote /t j/ respectively. The letter /t/ may be doubled, without any effect on its pronunciation. ‹tien yoc | cattin› /tjɛn jɔk | kaˈtɪn/ ‘yours husband | kitten’

The letter ‹c› is pronounced /ts/ before ‹e i y› and /k/ otherwise. The doubled ‹cc› is pronounced /kts/ before ‹e i y› and /k/ otherwise. ‹cigl cuivr bac | accaç occeir› /tsajl ˈkajv.r̩ bak | aˈkats ɔkˈtsir/ ‘darling copper twig | post kill’

The letter ‹g› may be pronounced as either of /g ʒ/ before ‹e i y› and is pronounced /g/ otherwise. The doubled ‹gg› may be pronounced as either of /g gʒ/ before ‹e i y› and is pronounced /g/ otherwise. ‹gevou genoil tenguð | suggesson› /geˈvu ʒeˈnɔjl tɛnˈgɪθ | ˌsɪg.ʒɛˈsɔn/ ‘{watch out} knee bauble | suggestion’

The letter ‹h› is either pronounced /h/ or is silent (the latter only in some vocabulary inherited from Latin and after vocalic ‹g›). ‹heu hom augher› /hau ɔm oˈjɛr/ ‘mien person tumbler’

The letter ‹s› is pronounced /z/ in coda position or between vowels, and /s/ otherwise or when doubled. ‹bevis bosaç bustr | bossir› /beˈvɪz boˈzats ˈbɪst.r̩ | bɔˈsɪr/ ‘banknote gunfire example | gentleman’ #### Consonant digraphs The six letters, ‹c g h s t y›, combine with other letters to form digraphs. The sequences ‹sc ch th› regularly denote the phonemes /x k θ/. The potentially ambiguous sequence ‹sch› is read /sk/. ‹sclar chym thral | schol› /xlar kɪm θral | skɔl/ ‘lightning lymph slave | school’

The sequence ‹cq› is pronounced /k/. ‹jalicq jacquot› /ʒaˈlɪk ʒaˈkwɔt/ ‘waistcoat coat’

title: Vowels
html:
Divide written vowels into two groups. Free vowels are either word-final or separated from the next vowel in the word by a single consonant letter (or one of ‹sc ch th›). ‹choma epu idiocy nothe› /koˈma eˈpi ˌi.djoˈtsi noˈθe/ ‘gnome express stupidity illegitimate’

Checked vowels are either in a final closed syllable or separated from the next vowel in the word by at least two consonant letters. ‹commun ambasctour sincer› /kɔˈmɪn ˌam.baxˈtur sɪnˈtsɛr/ ‘ordinary emissary genuine’

First consider the letters ‹a e i o u y› when they occur alone (not part of a digraph). When they are free, ‹a e i o u y› are pronounced /a e i o i i/. ‹cla vige bro cru try› /kla viˈʒe bro kri tri/ ‘key wealthy dude raw turn’

When they are checked, ‹a e i o u y› are pronounced /a ɛ ɪ ɔ ɪ ɪ/. ‹vindron vampyr effus› /vɪnˈdrɔn vamˈpɪr ɛˈfɪz/ ‘vintner vampire extensive’

Between a consonant and one of the vowels ‹a e o u›, the letters ‹i u› denote the semivowel /j/. The letter ‹u› denotes the semivowel /w/ specifically after ‹q›. ‹diac suet quelt› /djak sjɛt kwɛlt/ ‘mic familiar dinner’ #### Vowel digraphs The digraphs ‹au eu ou› are pronounced /o au u/ respectively. The rarer ‹ao eo› share their pronunciation with ‹eu›. ‹taur teun tout | paon theory› /tor taun tut | paun thauˈri/ ‘bull scant everything | peacock science’

The digraphs ‹ai ei oi ui› and ‹ay ey oy uy› are pronounced /e i ɔj aj/ respectively. Intervocalically ‹y› does double duty, simultaneously affecting the previous vowel as stated and acting as an onset /j/. ‹deit joivr | rayon huyaç› /dit ˈʒɔjv.r̩ | reˈjɔn haˈjats/ ‘finger frost | sunbeam corruption’

The vast majority of coda ‹g› and many other ‹g› before consonants form vowel digraphs. Here, the digraphs ‹ag eg ig og ug aug oug› are usually pronounced /ɛj ɪj aj ɔj aj oj uj/ respectively. ‹bag deg rig pog tug faug voug› /bɛj dɪj raj pɔj taj foj vuj/ ‘berry ten queue few passion sickle wrong’

However, there are exceptions where a ‹g› in one of the above environments is read consonantally. ‹magr magr | migrar scigrar› /ˈmag.r̩ ˈmɛj.r̩ | mɪˈgrar xajˈrar/ ‘rash scarce | migrate redistrict’

title: Phonemes
html:
Consonants

Borlish has a consonant inventory of middling size. Notable is the presence of /ʒ/ without its voiceless counterpart /ʃ/, due to a relatively recent sound change which backed the latter to /x/.

m   n      
p b   t d   k g  
    ts      
f v θ ð s z ʒ x h
w¹   l j r²  
  • ¹In native vocabulary the phoneme /w/ appears only after /k/. This, along with alternations like ‹voc voccar | bocq bocquar› /vɔk vɔˈkar | bɔk bɔˈkwar/ ‘summons {to summon} | fee {to charge}’

have led some to analyse /kw/ sequences as a single phoneme /kʷ/ which merges with /k/ in coda position. - ² The rhotic is uvular, but for ease we will denote it by /r/ in phonemic transcription. ### Vowels Borlish has eight monophthong vowel phonemes, typical of neighbouring Germanic and sister Gallo-Romance languages.

i ɪ u
e   o
ɛ a ɔ

Borlish also contains the diphthong /au/, as well as vowel+/j/ sequences that may be analysed as diphthongs.

The segments /m n r/ can appear as syllabic consonants. ‹batesm cohernt outrscac› /baˈtɛz.m̩ koˈhɛr.n̩t ˌut.r̩ˈxak/ ‘baptism consequent brazen’

The syllable preceding a syllabic consonant acts as a closed syllable, and the segment before a syllabic consonant acts as part of the coda of the previous syllable. Hence, it is usually considered most elegant to syllabify so that syllabic consonants have no onset.

title: Phonotactics
html:
There are several restrictions on where in a syllable or a word certain segments can appear. The consonants /v s ʒ h/ do not occur in coda position, and /ð/ does not occur word-finally. When morphology would place /ð s ʒ/ in a forbidden position they are replaced with /θ z x/ respectively. ‹nað-ar nað | fass-ar fas | naj-ar nascn› /naˈðar naθ | faˈsar faz | naˈʒar ˈnax.n̩/ ‘swim-inf swim.s | wrap-inf wrap.s | sail-inf sail.3p’

When morphology would place /v/ in a forbidden position, either /f/ replaces it or the preceding vowel is altered. ‹cav-ir caf | bav-ar bau› /kaˈvɪr kaf | baˈvar bo/ ‘get-inf get.s | bark-inf bark.s’

Syllabic consonants do not occur in stressed or initial syllables, and the ‘lax’ vowels /ɪ ɛ ɔ/ do not occur in stressed open syllables. ### Onsets Any vowel can begin an initial syllable, but under most analyses non-initial syllables must begin with a consonant. ‹al enç aiç il eir onc aust ou› /al ɛnts ets ɪl ir ɔnk ost u/ ‘wing start ease they go hook east egg’

Any single consonant may constitute an onset. Two-consonant onsets are of three kinds: for the first, the second segment is a liquid (one of /l j r/; the case of /w/ is dealt with above), preceded either by a stop or a fricative. The liquid /l/ does not occur after the segments /t d θ ð ʒ/, and the liquid /r/ does not occur after the segments /ð s z ʒ/. ‹plait droug gien thron› /plet druj ʒjɛn θrɔn/ ‘pleased contribution cheek throne’

The second class of two-consonant onsets are a heterorganic stop followed by one of /s θ t d/; these usually derive from Greek. ‹xyron chthonic ptyssoscia bdella› /ksiˈrɔn kθɔˈnɪk ˌpti.soˈxja bdɛˈla/ ‘scalpel subterraneous glaucoma virus’

The remaining two-consonant onsets comprise /s/ followed by a nasal, a stop, the two liquids /l j/ or the two fricatives /f v/. ‹smout skore sdegn slanc siesc sforç svam› /smut skoˈre sdɪjn slank sjɛx sfɔrts svam/ ‘riot urgent scorn thin siege strain sponge’

All the possible three-consonant onsets are formed by the combination of /s/+consonant and consonant+liquid onsets. ‹splorar spien sdruçol sfrey› /sploˈrar spjɛn sdriˈtsɔl sfri/ ‘explore hope lubrication horror’

No more complex onsets are permitted. ### Codas Empty codas are permitted, including word-finally. However, the ‘lax’ vowels /ɪ ɛ ɔ/ do not occur in final open syllables (as these would necessarily be stressed). ‹idone nijomm-au› /ˌi.doˈne ˌni.ʒɔˈmo/ ‘suitable bind-pst’

Any single consonant except /v s ʒ h/ may be a coda. There are several types of two-consonant coda. A liquid may be followed by any non-liquid that can appear in codas at all; a nasal by any such non-liquid and non-nasal. ‹sculd tragç corf lucern | hamt coyenç ans› /xɪld trɛjts kɔrf liˈtsɛrn | hamt koˈjɛnts anz/ ‘obligation clue raven lantern | live.s prevention handle’

The fricative /s/ may precede a voiceless stop; /f/ may precede /t/ specifically. ‹cosp fest masq | haft› /kɔsp fɛst mask | haft/ ‘{bee sting} banquet disguise | busy’

Conversely, /s/ may also follow any voiceless stop; after /t/ this is identical to the affricate phoneme /ts/. ‹laps ouç jex› /laps uts ʒɛks/ ‘mistake silence bullseye’

Finally among two-consonant codas, the cluster /kt/ is also permitted. ‹abject› /abˈʒɛkt/ ‘despicable’

The few three-consonant codas that appear are a nasal or /j/ with a licit two-consonant cluster. ‹boist deunx defonct› /bɔjst daunks deˈfɔnkt/ ‘box {near miss} deceased’

No more complex codas are permitted.

title: Stress
html:
The final syllable is usually stressed. ‹scadom tatover› /xaˈdɔm ˌta.toˈvɛr/ ‘tomato potato’

In words of four or more syllables, secondary stress is usually assigned in iambs, although anapaestic patterns do occur when the interior syllables are sufficiently light. ‹deborsellar haubrjoranç› /deˌbɔr.sɛˈlar ˌhob.r̩.ʒoˈrants/ ‘pickpocket advertisement’

There are some exceptions to final stress. Syllabic consonants reject stress onto the previous syllable. Moreover, the suffixes -(e)nt (forming present participles of -r conjugation verbs), -(e)nç (forming nouns from the same verbs) and -essem, -errem (forming comparatives) also enforce penultimate stress. ‹casn pardenç pessem veðerrem› /ˈkazn̩ ˈpar.dɛnts ˈpɛ.sɛm veˈðɛ.rɛm/ ‘oak loss worse elder’

In many dialects these suffixes are parsimoniously analysed as instead containing syllabic consonants, pronounced as /-n̩t -n̩ts -ɛs.m̩ -ɛr.m̩/.

title: Merch
html:
Merch is an English language of the German family of languages which evolved on the island of Albion. It is closely related to other English languages like Kentish and Wessern, and is the primary language of the polity of Markland.

Contemporary Merch descends from the Early Merch spoken in Markland, which itself descends from Anglian varieties of Old English.

This article principally describes the acrolect of Merch spoken in and around the capital city of Tommarth. ## Phonology ### Consonants The consonant phonemes found in Merch are /m n p t tʃ k b d dʒ g f θ s ʃ h v ð z w l ɾ j/ and they are subject to the following orthographic conventions: - b d f h k l m n p t v w z represent their IPA symbols. - j r y represent /dʒ ɾ j/ respectively. - c usually represents /s/ before i e y and /k/ elsewhere. - awice /əˈwəis/ “indeed” < OE ġewiss “certain” - coholl /kʊˈhɔl/ “sulfide” < Ar كُحْل “kohl” - g usually represents /g/ but may represent /dʒ/ before i e y. - eager /ˈeː.gə/ “acid” < L ācer - nargill /ˈnaː.dʒɪl/ “coconut” < Ar نَرْجِيل “coconut palm” - girl /geːl/ “young person” - q appears almost always in the diagraph qu to represent /kw/. - quoit /kwəit/ “tyre, torus” - s represents /z/ between vowels and word-finally (unless doubled), and /s/ otherwise. - Brasil /bɾəˈzɪl/ “Newfoundland” < Ir Breasail - betimes /bɪˈtəimz/ “earlier” - surblavick /soːˈblɛː.vɪk/ “ultraviolet” < L blavus “blue” - x represents /gz/ between vowels, and /ks/ otherwise. - oxy /ˈɔg.ziː/ “stubborn” - œculux /ˈɛ.kjəˌlʊks/ “EM radiation” < Gr οἶκος “house”, L lux “light”

There are several digraphs of letters with -h: ch gh ph sh th wh. - ch usually represents /tʃ/, but sometimes also /k/. - chalk /tʃalk/ “chalk” - yacht /jakt/ “cult” < Du jaght “hunting party” - gh usually represents either /ʃ/ (following i) or /f/ (following u). - wight /wɪʃt/ “person” < OE wiht “thing, creature” - ough /uːf/ “bad” < OE wōh “wrong, crooked” - NB i’ght /ˈəift/ “I must”, contracted from I ought - ph represents /f/. - aphlox /ˈa.flɔks/ “carbon dioxide” < Gr ά- “without” and φλόξ “flame” - sh represents /ʃ/. - shake /ʃɛːk/ “shake” - th usually represents either of /θ ð/, and sometimes /t/. - thrift /θɾɪft/ “energy” < ON þrift “prosperity” - yeather /ˈjeːðə/ “together” < OE geador - wh represents /f/. - white /fəit/ “white” There are several situations in which written consonants may be silent. - In certain endings: participle -end /ən/, infinitive -en /ə/, superlative -est /əs/ (in all three cases the final consonant is however pronounced before a vowel-initial word in the same clause). - discurrend /dɪˈskʊ.ɾən/ “different” < L discurrēns “roaming” - chammen /ˈtʃa.mə/ “to chew” (poss. onomatopoetic) - tharvest /ˈθaː.vəs/ “least sociable” < OE þeorf “unleavened” - Final -mn and -mb are pronounced /m/. - Coda r lengthens the preceding vowel and word-final -er is pronounced /ə/. - interpel /ɪnˈtɛː.pəl/ “interact with” < L interpellō “I disturb” - sunder /ˈsʊn.də/ “many” < OE sundor “separately” - Post-tonic -st- immediately before a closed syllable is pronounced /s/. - hirstend /ˈheː.sən/ “extra” < OE hyrstan “to ornament” - pistol /ˈpɪ.səl/ “message” < L epistola - Likewise, post-tonic -v- is lost in some common words. - morover /mʊˈɾoː(ə)/ “as well” - ever /ɛː(ə)/ “always” ### Vowels The vowels of (this variety of) Merch comprise the short vowels: /a ɛ ɪ ɔ ʊ ə/ the long vowels: /aː ɛː eː iː oː uː/ and two diphthongs: /əi əu/ which are subject to the following orthographic conventions: - In stressed syllables, “short” a e i~y o u represent /a ɛ ɪ ɔ ʊ/ (noting that schwa cannot be stressed). - samn /sam/ “set, collection” < OE samnian “gather” - whelp /fɛlp/ “baby animal” - quick /kwɪk/ “awake” - thon /ðɔn/ “one, someone”, contracted from the one - bulk /bʊlk/ “cargo” - In unstressed syllables, a e i~y o u instead represent /ə ə~ɪ ɪ ʊ ʊ/—except word-finally, where /ɪ ʊ/ become /iː ə/. - defectend /dɪˈfɛk.tən/ “positively charged” - behemoth /bɪˈhiː.mʊθ/ “hippopotamus” - costumery /kʊˈstjəu.mə.ɾiː/ “fashion plate” - “Long” a e i~y o u represent /ɛː iː əi uː jəu/. - besake /bɪˈzɛːk/ “because” - mete /miːt/ “standard, gauge” < OE metan “measure” - ty /təi/ “join” - poke /puːk/ “container” < OFr poque “bag” - huge /hjəudʒ/ “huge” There are plenty of digraphs representing vowels. - Coda -r: ar er ir or ur represent /aː ɛː eː oː oː/. - davarn /dəˈvaːn/ “grand hotel, resort” < W tafarn “inn” - -a: ea oa represent /eː oː/. - road /ˈɾoːd/ “travel” - -e: ee ie ue represent /iː əi jəu/. - conspue /kʊnˈspjəu/ “to deride” < L cōnspuō “I spit upon” - -i/y: ai ei oi and ay ey oy represent /əi əi~iː əi/. - fain /fəin/ “willing(ly)” - -o: oo represents /uː/. - soon /suːn/ “as soon as” - -u/w: au eu ou and aw ew ow represent /əu əu əu~uː/. - blew /bləu/ “blue” - coshow /ˈkɔ.ʃuː/ “rubber” < Quechua kawchu ## Examples

A jalicked knave bought a new quister. /ə ˈdʒa.lɪ.kɪd knɛːv bɔft ə nəu ˈkwɪ.stə/ A guy in a tuxedo bought a new phone.

Aquifex is the temendest corporal. /ˈa.kwɪˌfɛks ɪz ðe ˈtiː.mən.dəs ˈkoː.pʊ.ɾəl/
Hydrogen is the most abundant element.

Do thy breches of and put thysen abed! /duː ðiː ˈbɾiː.tʃəz ɔf ən pʊt ðɪˈzɛn əˈbɛd/ Take off your clothes and get in the bed!

Way o’dreing al tharin ough, he asked “As sutch?” /wəi əˈdɾiː.ɪŋ‿gal ðəˈɾɪn uːf | hiː ˈa.skəd | az sʊtʃ/
While doing everything wrong, he asked “Like that?”

Psolick tersures may breken wines brewing of. /ˈsuː.lɪk ˈtɛː.zjəz məi ˈbɾiːkə wəinz ˈbɾəwɪŋg ɔf/ Sulphites can stop wine fermentation.
